a.xlink:title: Final synthesis
Google DeepMind's Co-Scientist (Nature 2026) generated and refined biomedical hypotheses, with AML drug-repurposing candidates validated in vitro.
2026 has crossed the autonomy threshold; the disclosure unit must catch up.
Sakana AI's end-to-end pipeline (Nature 2026 651:914-919) produced a manuscript that passed first-round blind workshop peer review.
A clinician without large-lab affiliation can in 2026 assemble an equivalent workflow on commodity tooling.
Current policy permits "readability and language improvements only".
Current biomedical AI policies are calibrated for 2023 LLM use and structurally lag 2026 practice.
Current policy prohibits using AI to generate scientific arguments, draft methodology, write literature reviews, or create new content.

a.xlink:title: Current policy protects against three real harms — authorship inflation, scientific hallucination, undocumented intervention — but prohibition without audit invites camouflage rather than mitigation.

The Perspective itself is produced under Disclosure 2.0; every artefact the manifest requires is publicly archived; the supporting null and the audit findings are visible.

a.xlink:title: Disclosure 2.0 preserves the hallucination verbatim and records resolution in subsequent commits, making the failure mode catchable rather than camouflaged.
As written the policy declares the 2026 workflow in violation; the realistic equilibrium under such a policy is non-compliance or non-use, both worse than updating the mechanism.

a.xlink:title: Fully autonomous and copilot-supervised are two legitimate equilibria with different cost-of-error profiles; Disclosure 2.0 is the disclosure layer both should share.

The minimum viable manifest (prompts plus model identifier plus commit hash plus tagged release) covers the consequential audit surfaces at near-zero infrastructure cost and is the recommended on-ramp.
a.text: plus model identifier plus commit hash

a.xlink:title: The manifest's models array supports cross-model audit (Layer 1 on one vendor, Layer 2 on another); same-vendor audits are equivalent to a single-rater study.

a.xlink:title: At end-of-life, manifest compliance requires either re-execution on a named successor model with a divergent-attestation badge, or freezing the artefact in the audit log; transcripts, code, and manuscripts survive deprecation.
